scenarioName: Unstable Bradycardia — Complete Heart Block, Atropine-Refractory, Transcutaneous Pacing

scenarioStory.history: 82-year-old retired electrician was watching the evening news in his recliner. He stood up to go to the bathroom and made it about three steps before his wife saw him 'go gray' and crumple to the carpet. He was unresponsive for approximately 20–30 seconds, did not have any seizure-like activity, and did not strike his head (rug-covered floor; she heard the fall but the recliner blocked a hard impact). When he came around he was confused for a few minutes, then oriented but exhausted and pale. His wife checked his radial pulse and found it 'really slow.' He has felt 'off' for two days — fatigue, exertional shortness of breath while taking the trash out, and one earlier 'gray-out' that he didn't tell anyone about. Denies chest pain, denies palpitations. He has CAD post-CABG ×3 (15 years ago), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and chronic kidney disease (stage 3). Of note, his cardiologist increased his metoprolol succinate from 25 mg to 50 mg about three weeks ago for atrial ectopy. He took his usual evening dose tonight.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS finds an 82-year-old man on the carpet, alert but pale and clearly ill. Initial monitor connection shows third-degree AV block — atrial rate ~85 with completely independent ventricular escape rate of 28. BP is 78/42 with cool extremities and mild confusion. The clinical picture is unstable bradycardia from complete heart block, likely worsened by his recent metoprolol dose escalation on top of age-related conduction disease. Atropine will be tried per protocol but is unlikely to work in CHB because the block is below the AV node — the teaching point is to PREPARE TCP at the same time atropine is being drawn, not after it has failed. → Atropine ineffective; TCP with sedation: Atropine 1 mg IV given — no change in rate or rhythm, as expected for infranodal block. Crew explains TCP to the patient and wife. Sedation/analgesia given before pacing: fentanyl 80 mcg IV (80 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 80 mcg, max 100 mcg) plus midazolam 1 mg IV slow push (small initial increment given age 82, hypotension, and elderly dose reduction). Pacer started at 70 bpm and the output (mA) titrated upward; mechanical capture confirmed at 70 mA with palpable femoral and radial pulses corresponding 1:1 to pacer spikes. BP rises to 102/64 and mentation clears within 30–60 seconds. → Capture confirmed; transport on TCP: Patient transported with continuous transcutaneous pacing to a PCI- and electrophysiology-capable receiving hospital. Mechanical capture continuously confirmed by palpation of femoral pulse synchronous with pacer spikes. Sedation/analgesia titrated as needed for pacing discomfort during transport. Pad sites monitored. Bedside handoff includes initial rhythm, atropine attempt, joules-equivalent (mA), capture threshold, sedation given, and recent metoprolol dose increase as a likely contributor.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Syncope while walking from his recliner to the bathroom about 20 minutes ago; came to on the floor confused, now lightheaded, weak, with a 'really slow' pulse felt by his wife.

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS finds an 82-year-old man on the carpet, alert but pale and clearly ill. Initial monitor connection shows third-degree AV block — atrial rate ~85 with completely independent ventricular escape rate of 28. BP is 78/42 with cool extremities and mild confusion. The clinical picture is unstable bradycardia from complete heart block, likely worsened by his recent metoprolol dose escalation on top of age-related conduction disease. Atropine will be tried per protocol but is unlikely to work in CHB because the block is below the AV node — the teaching point is to PREPARE TCP at the same time atropine is being drawn, not after it has failed.
relatedChecklist: Primary survey; identify instability — AMS + hypotension + CHB = UNSTABLE bradycardia
Cardiac monitor; pacer pads on (TCP pre-armed); 12-lead EKG
IV access ×2 large-bore (atropine, sedation, push-dose epi)
Check blood glucose — rule out hypoglycemia driver
Atropine 1 mg IV; recognize CHB unlikely to respond → prepare TCP simultaneously

scenarioEvents.name: Atropine ineffective; TCP with sedation
scenarioEvents.description: Atropine 1 mg IV given — no change in rate or rhythm, as expected for infranodal block. Crew explains TCP to the patient and wife. Sedation/analgesia given before pacing: fentanyl 80 mcg IV (80 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 80 mcg, max 100 mcg) plus midazolam 1 mg IV slow push (small initial increment given age 82, hypotension, and elderly dose reduction). Pacer started at 70 bpm and the output (mA) titrated upward; mechanical capture confirmed at 70 mA with palpable femoral and radial pulses corresponding 1:1 to pacer spikes. BP rises to 102/64 and mentation clears within 30–60 seconds.
relatedChecklist: Fentanyl 80 mcg IV (80 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) — analgesia before pacing
Midazolam 1 mg slow IV — anxiolysis (reduced increment for age 82, hypotension)
Transcutaneous pacing at 70 bpm; titrate mA up; confirm electrical AND mechanical capture
Allergies, BP, SpO2, capnography on; reassess pain/RR every 5 min during pacing
Push-dose epi 10 mcg IV ready (1 mL of 10 mcg/mL dilution) if BP fails to recover with capture

scenarioEvents.name: Capture confirmed; transport on TCP
scenarioEvents.description: Patient transported with continuous transcutaneous pacing to a PCI- and electrophysiology-capable receiving hospital. Mechanical capture continuously confirmed by palpation of femoral pulse synchronous with pacer spikes. Sedation/analgesia titrated as needed for pacing discomfort during transport. Pad sites monitored. Bedside handoff includes initial rhythm, atropine attempt, joules-equivalent (mA), capture threshold, sedation given, and recent metoprolol dose increase as a likely contributor.
relatedChecklist: Reassess capture continuously: pulse 1:1 with spikes; titrate mA if loses capture
Titrate sedation/analgesia for pacing comfort; reassess RR + capnography q5 min
Transport to PCI/EP-capable hospital; bedside handoff with rhythm strips + dose timeline

scenarioEvents.name: TCP started without sedation
scenarioEvents.description: Crew started TCP without giving any pre-pacing sedation or analgesia. Capture is achieved electrically and mechanically, BP improves — but the alert patient experiences each pacing impulse as a severe muscular jolt and shouts in pain. He becomes agitated, tries to remove the pads, and his wife is distressed at the bedside. The protocol explicitly states 'If pacing is performed, consider sedation or pain control' — this is not optional comfort care but a safety/efficacy issue, because an agitated patient who fights the pacer can lose capture and pull off the pads. The crew now must give analgesia and anxiolysis post-hoc and de-escalate while maintaining capture.
relatedChecklist: Fentanyl 80 mcg IV NOW (80 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) for pacing pain
Midazolam 1 mg slow IV — anxiolysis (reduced increment for age 82)
Verbal reassurance; explain procedure to patient and wife; non-pharmacologic measures
Recheck pad adhesion; confirm continued capture; transport without losing pacing
Document plan: pre-pacing sedation/analgesia in future TCP; QI debrief
